3-Day Intensive Course

Agentic AI
for Developers

From LLM completions to production-grade multi-agent systems.

3
Days
9
Sessions
6
Topologies
5
Sens-AI Habits

Day One

LLM Based Agents

From stateless completions to autonomous reasoning loops — agent fundamentals, frameworks, tools, ReAct, planning, and RAG.

What is an Agent? LangGraph Ollama ReAct Plan & Execute RAG + Ragas

Session 1.1

LLM vs Agent

LLM (completion)
Input → Model → Output Stateless. One shot. Forgets everything. You send a prompt, it generates text.
Agent
Perceive → Reason → Act → Observe → ↻ Stateful loop. Has tools. Accumulates context. Decides what to do next.
The PRAO Loop

Perception → Reasoning → Action → Observation — the agent repeats this until done or stopped.

Session 1.1 · The Differentiator

The PRAO Loop — what turns an LLM into an Agent

LLM alone = just R

One Reasoning step. No senses, no hands, no feedback. prompt → text and it's over. Cannot check its own work.

Agent = LLM + Loop + Tools

Same LLM, now embedded in P→R→A→O. It can fetch data, call tools, see results, and retry — adapting on every turn.

Session 1.1 · Foundations

Classic Agent Types Russell & Norvig · AIMA

  • Simple Reflex — maps current percept directly to action via condition–action rules. No memory, no world model. Works only in fully observable environments. e.g. thermostat, spam filter.
  • Model-Based Reflex — keeps internal state to track what it cannot currently see. A world model fills gaps in perception. e.g. vacuum robot remembering cleaned rooms.
  • Goal-Based — acts to reach explicit goals. Considers future consequences. Search and planning emerge here. e.g. GPS routing, chess engine.
  • Utility-Based — picks the action with highest expected utility. Trades off competing goals — speed vs cost vs safety. e.g. ride-share dispatcher, autonomous driving.
  • Learning — improves over time via a critic, learning element, and problem generator. Any of the above can become a learning agent. e.g. AlphaGo, RLHF-tuned LLMs.

Session 1.1 · Architecture

Simple Reflex Agent

Key idea

A lookup table from percept to action. The fastest possible agent — and the most fragile. Loops forever if the same percept yields the wrong action.

Real example

Thermostat, regex-based spam filter, deterministic form validator, rate-limit middleware.

Session 1.1 · Architecture

Model-Based Reflex Agent

Key idea

Adds an internal world model so the agent can act in partially observable environments. State is updated each tick from the percept and the last action.

Real example

Roomba mapping cleaned rooms, conversational chatbot with short-term memory, TCP retransmit logic.

Session 1.1 · Architecture

Goal-Based Agent

Key idea

Action chosen by simulating outcomes against an explicit goal. Search and planning live here. Slower than reflex — but flexible when goals change.

Real example

GPS routing, chess engine minimax, build-system dependency resolver, plan-and-execute LLM agents.

Session 1.1 · Architecture

Utility-Based Agent

Key idea

Generalises goals to a continuous utility function. Trades off competing objectives — speed, cost, safety — and handles uncertainty via expected utility.

Real example

Ride-share dispatcher, autonomous-driving cost map, LLM-judge model routing (cheap vs accurate vs fast).

Session 1.1 · Architecture

Learning Agent

Key idea

A meta-architecture that wraps any of the prior four. The Critic compares behaviour to a fixed standard; the Learning Element edits the Performance Element accordingly.

Real example

AlphaGo (self-play), RLHF-tuned LLMs, eval-driven prompt tuning, online recommender systems with bandit exploration.

Session 1.1 · Foundations

The Capability Ladder

Reflex + state Model-Based + goals Goal-Based + preferences Utility-Based + adaptation Learning
Each type adds one capability the previous one lacked. Pick the simplest type that solves the problem.
Why this still matters

The lens is universal. An LLM agent is, in classic terms, a learning utility-based agent where language is both percept and action.

The cardinal sin

Reaching for "learning" or "multi-agent" when a reflex rule would do. Complexity must be earned, not assumed.

Session 1.1 · Foundations

Classic Theory → LLM Era

Classic typeLLM-era manifestationExample
Simple ReflexStateless prompt → completionInline code suggestion
Model-Based ReflexConversation memory + tool callsReAct loop, Claude Code
Goal-BasedPlan-and-execute agentsDevin, AutoGPT
Utility-BasedCost / latency / quality routingModel routing, judge-based selection
LearningRLHF, in-context learning, eval-driven loopsRLHF-tuned models, prompt-tuning harnesses
PEAS still applies

Before building, specify Performance measure, Environment, Actuators (tools), Sensors (inputs). The vocabulary is 30 years old and still correct.

Session 1.2

Framework Landscape

FrameworkStrengthUse When
LangGraph ★Explicit state graphs, checkpoints, HITLProduction agents needing control flow
CrewAIDeclarative role-based teamsQuick prototypes, role-driven workflows
AutoGenMulti-agent natural language turnsResearch, debate, collaborative tasks
LlamaIndexEvent-driven Workflows, 300+ data connectors, best-in-class RAG & indexingData/RAG-first agents — doc Q&A, knowledge bases, structured extraction
Google ADKMulti-language (Py/TS/Go/Java), Session/State/Memory, A2A nativeProduction agents on Vertex AI / Gemini Enterprise
AWS StrandsModel-driven loop, MCP-native, OTel observabilityLean agents on AWS (Lambda, Bedrock AgentCore)
Hosted: Claude API Local: Ollama (llama3.2, qwen2.5-coder)

Session 1.2

Tool Design Principles

  • Precise names — query_database not query. The name is the model's primary routing signal.
  • Docstring as contract — describe what it does, each arg, what it returns, and hard constraints.
  • Structured returns — return dicts, not raw strings. The model reasons about structure better than prose.
  • Validate up front — reject bad input immediately with a clear message. The model will read it and self-correct.
  • Scope constraints — never give an agent more access than it needs. Least privilege applies.

Session 1.2

ReAct Agent

User message → LLM reasons → Tool call → Observation → LLM reasons → Final answer
Repeat until no tool calls (end_turn) or MAX_ITERATIONS reached.
Max Iterations

Always set a hard cap (e.g. 15). Without it, the agent loops forever when stuck. Track iteration_count in state.

Error Classes

Hallucinated tool — explain & list valid names.
Policy block — block, do not retry.
Transient error — explain, let agent retry.

Session 1.3

Plan & Execute

ReAct
Figures out the next step only after seeing the previous result. Reactive. Good for exploratory tasks.
Plan & Execute
Generates full plan before first action. Replans on failure. Separates strategic thinking from execution.
The split

Planner (claude-sonnet, called once) → decomposes into 3–7 steps.
Executor (claude-haiku + tools, called per step) → mini ReAct loop per step.
Replanner → triggered on step failure, generates new plan from that point.

Session 1.3

RAG for Agents

Query → Embed → Vector search → Top-K chunks → Inject → LLM
In agents: retrieval is a tool — the agent decides when to call it, what to search, and how many results.
Knowledge Graph

Stores entities + relationships. Enables structured traversal queries. Complements vector search where relationships matter.

Ragas Evaluation

Faithfulness — hallucination detection.
Context Precision — retrieval relevance.
Context Recall — retrieval completeness.
Answer Relevancy — response quality.

Session 1.3 · Evaluation

Ragas — How RAG Evaluation Actually Works

Ship it · put it in CI

result = evaluate(dataset, metrics=[faithfulness, answer_relevancy, context_precision, context_recall]) — three metrics need no ground truth, so you can run them on live traffic. Fail the build when Faithfulness drops below your threshold; the hallucination never ships.

Session 1.3 · Knowledge Graph

When Vector RAG Hits a Wall

The fundamental difference

Vector RAG retrieves what is similar. Knowledge Graphs retrieve what is related. Multi-hop queries — "X of Y of Z" — are where similarity quietly fails and relations win.

Session 1.3 · Knowledge Graph

Six Wins for Knowledge Graphs

DimensionVector RAGKnowledge Graph
Multi-hop reasoningBrittle — synthesis across chunksNative — graph traversal
ExplainabilityOpaque cosine scoresThe path is the answer
DeterminismEmbedding drift between modelsExact traversal — same query, same path
SchemaNone — free textTyped entities & relations
AggregationsVery limitedCount, group, filter, shortest-path
Phantom entitiesHallucination riskOnly existing nodes can be returned
Best-fit domains

Org charts, supply chains, compliance & regulation, code dependencies, drug interactions, fraud rings — anywhere relations are the data.

The trade-off

Index-time cost rises — entity extraction (LLM-driven) and schema discipline are required. Setup days, not minutes.

Session 1.3 · Knowledge Graph

GraphRAG — The Hybrid Pattern

Use both when

Relational queries on top of an unstructured corpus, audit-required answers, "list / count / who-related-to-whom" questions over enterprise knowledge.

Stick with vector RAG when

Pure semantic search, long-form prose Q&A, fast iteration, no clear entity schema. Don't pay the GraphRAG tax for similarity-only problems.

Session 1.3 · Chunking

Why Chunking Matters

Too small

Snippets ripped from context. Retrieval finds the right line but the LLM has nothing to reason with.

Too large

Multiple topics averaged into one embedding vector. Cosine similarity drops, retrieval misses, irrelevant text fills the context window.

Wrong boundary

Cuts mid-sentence or mid-code-block. Embedding distorted, LLM sees grammatically broken text.

Session 1.3 · Chunking · Strategy 1

Fixed-Size + Overlap

When to use

Plain unstructured text, demos, baseline benchmarks. Fast and predictable — sometimes "good enough" is the right answer.

Watch out for

Cuts code blocks, tables, JSON, and Markdown headings in half. Storage cost grows linearly with overlap percentage.

Session 1.3 · Chunking · Strategy 2

Recursive Character Splitting

When to use

Default for prose: docs, articles, blog posts, support tickets. Respects natural boundaries opportunistically — almost always beats fixed-size.

Watch out for

Default separator list is English-centric. Code, JSON, and structured formats need a custom list — Language.PYTHON, Language.MARKDOWN.

Session 1.3 · Chunking · Strategy 3

Document-Aware Chunking

When to use

Markdown, HTML, source code, RST, JSON. Anywhere structure exists, exploit it — the boundaries were already labelled by the author.

Watch out for

Sections vary wildly in size — combine with a size-based splitter inside each section. Useless on plain prose.

Session 1.3 · Chunking · Strategy 4

Semantic Chunking

When to use

Long-form technical writing where topics shift mid-section: research papers, transcripts, mixed-topic blog posts. Quality over speed.

Watch out for

Slow — embeds every sentence at index time. Threshold tuning required. Falls back to recursive splitting if all sentences look similar.

Session 1.3 · Chunking · Strategy 5

Parent-Child & What's Next

When to use

When small chunks improve retrieval precision but the LLM needs surrounding context. ParentDocumentRetriever (LangChain), SmallToBigRetriever (LlamaIndex).

Advanced (2024+)

Contextual Retrieval (Anthropic) — prepend an LLM-generated context blurb to each chunk; ~49% retrieval-failure reduction.
Late Chunking (Jina AI) — embed the whole doc with a long-context model, then chunk the embeddings.

Day Two

Single Agent Mastery

AI-First development mindset, Sens-AI habits, MCP, context engineering, four memory types, observability, and guardrails.

Sens-AI Framework MCP Context Engineering 4 Memory Types Langfuse Guardrails

Session 2.1

AI-First Software Development

The rehash loop — key failure mode

AI keeps generating slight variations of the same wrong answer. It's not a prompt problem — the AI has exhausted the context you gave it. Stop prompting. Apply the Sens-AI habits.

Traditional dev

Developer writes code → runs tests → ships.

AI-First dev

Developer provides strategic intent + framing + judgment. Agent handles mechanical execution.

Session 2.1 · Sens-AI Framework

Five Habits Andrew Stellman · O'Reilly 2025

Context → Research → Framing → Refining → Critical Thinking
Critical Thinking reveals what Context was missing — the loop restarts, compounding quality over time.
01
Context

What does AI need to know?

02
Research

Deepen your own understanding

03
Framing

Define what you're solving

04
Refining

Iterate deliberately

05
Critical Thinking

Judge before you trust

Session 2.1 · Sens-AI Framework

Habits in Practice

#HabitThe question to askKey anti-pattern
01 ContextSupply code, intent, constraints, what you've tried"What does AI need that it doesn't have?"Thin prompt: "fix the bug"
02 ResearchUse AI + docs + codebase to you understand better"Do I understand this enough to guide AI?"Prompting out of a stuck loop
03 FramingWhat you want + constraints + don'ts + definition of done"Am I solving the right problem, precisely?"Vague: "make it more testable"
04 RefiningRead → diagnose specifically → one targeted change"What was wrong, and what one thing do I change?"Random tweaks → vibe coding
05 CriticalGenerate alternatives, use AI as its own critic, 6-month test"Is this correct and will it hold up?"Accepting first output passively

Session 2.1

Rules, Commands & Skills

Rules
Operating Constraints

Defined in CLAUDE.md. Role, capabilities, constraints, escalation rules. Loaded every session. Applies Sens-AI Context habit upfront.

Commands
Predefined Workflows

Slash commands: /review /test /migrate /doc. Pre-engineered prompts for recurring tasks. Applies Sens-AI Framing habit.

Skills
Reusable Sub-procedures

Self-contained agent workflows with typed input/output contracts. Include a self-evaluation step. Applies Sens-AI Critical Thinking habit.

Sub-agents: Local vs Cloud

Local — in-process LangGraph graph, zero network, shared memory.
Cloud — HTTP delegation to a deployed agent; returns when complete.

Session 2.1 · Harness Engineering

Harness Engineering — Agent = Model + Harness

The trust gap, made explicit

LLMs are non-deterministic, don't know your codebase, and think in tokens — not in your domain. The harness is everything around the model that closes that gap: the rules, skills, MCPs, linters, tests, and review agents that steer it before it acts and catch it after. Rules, commands & skills aren't the harness — they're its building blocks.

Birgitta Böckeler · martinfowler.com/articles/harness-engineering · Apr 2026

Session 2.1 · Harness Engineering

Two Axes — Direction & Execution

You need all four cells

Feedforward-only and the agent never finds out whether the rules worked. Feedback-only and it repeats the same mistakes. Computational catches structure cheaply; inferential catches semantics expensively. The strongest harness mixes all four.

FEEDFORWARD · COMPUTATIONAL
Deterministic guides

Type systems, language servers, .editorconfig, codemods, scaffolding scripts, framework conventions. Cheap, reliable, every run. Reduces the variety the agent can produce in the first place.

FEEDFORWARD · INFERENTIAL
LLM-readable guides

AGENTS.md · CLAUDE.md · skills · how-tos · architecture docs · MCPs that surface team knowledge. Slower & non-deterministic, but encode intent the type system can't.

FEEDBACK · COMPUTATIONAL
Deterministic sensors

Linters, type-checkers, unit tests, ArchUnit, dep-cruiser, mutation testing, coverage gates. Best when their messages are written for the agent — embed the fix instructions in the error.

FEEDBACK · INFERENTIAL
Semantic sensors

Review agents, LLM-as-judge, response-quality samplers, log-anomaly detectors, "garbage collection" agents that scan for drift. Expensive — schedule them off the hot path.

Session 2.1 · Harness Engineering

Three Things to Regulate — and how mature the tooling is

MATURITY · HIGH
Maintainability Harness

Internal code quality: duplication, complexity, coverage, style, drift. Decades of pre-existing tooling — linters, type-checkers, test runners, ArchUnit. Plug them in and they pay back immediately.

MATURITY · EMERGING
Architecture Fitness Harness

Non-functional characteristics: performance, observability, security posture, resilience. Encoded as fitness functions — perf-test deltas, log-quality skills, dependency rules. Workable, but mostly bespoke per team.

MATURITY · OPEN PROBLEM
Behaviour Harness

Does the app actually do what the user wanted? Today: spec in, AI-written tests out — too much faith in AI-generated tests. Approved-fixtures and human-curated acceptance suites help, but the gap is real. This is where supervision still earns its keep.

Harnessability is a property of your codebase

Strong types, clear module boundaries, mature frameworks → easy to harness. Untyped, monolithic, legacy → the harness is hardest to build exactly where it's most needed. Greenfield teams should design for harnessability on day one.

Session 2.1 · Harness Engineering

The Steering Loop — Keep Quality Left

GUIDES (feedforward) SENSORS (feedback) SCOPE ───────────────────── ──────────────────── ───── AGENTS.md, skills, LSP ─▶ AGENT ─▶ linters, type-check, PRE-COMMIT ref docs, codemods unit tests, /code-review cheap · every change │ ▼ fixes self-applied human review PRE-INTEGRATION │ judgment · social accountability ▼ ─── INTEGRATION ─── │ ▼ rerun fast sensors POST-INTEGRATION + mutation tests, /detailed-review broader · slower + /architecture-review │ ▼ CONTINUOUS DRIFT & HEALTH OFF-PATH /find-dead-code · dependabot run on a schedule /coverage-quality · log-anomalies feeds new commits SLO watchers · janitor agents
Spread checks by cost

Fast & cheap → run on every keystroke. Slow & expensive → run post-integration. The earlier a sensor fires, the cheaper the fix — same logic as continuous integration, applied to agent output.

Iterate the harness, not just the prompt

When the same mistake recurs, don't re-prompt — add a guide or a sensor. Coding agents themselves make custom linters and structural tests cheap to write. Steering is the human's real job.

Aim humans at the hard part

A good harness doesn't replace human review — it redirects it. Computational sensors handle structure; inferential sensors handle most semantics; humans handle correctness, intent, and trade-offs.

Session 2.1

Model Context Protocol (MCP)

What it solves

Before MCP: every agent needed custom integration code per tool. After MCP: write a tool server once, any compatible agent uses it — including Claude Code.

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ MCP Client (Agent) MCP Server (Tool Provider) │ │ ─────────────────── ───────────────────────── │ │ Claude Code ←──→ Filesystem server │ │ Custom LangGraph ←──→ GitHub server │ │ Any MCP client ←──→ PostgreSQL server │ │ ←──→ Your custom server │ │ │ │ JSON-RPC 2.0 over stdio / HTTP+SSE │ └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Session 2.1 · MCP Architecture

Three Roles, One Protocol

HOST

The AI app the user runs. Owns the LLM, UI, consent, and one or more clients.

CLIENT

Lives inside the host. Holds a single 1:1 connection to one server. Speaks JSON-RPC.

SERVER

Standalone process. Exposes tools, resources, prompts. Local or remote.

Session 2.1 · The Host

MCP Host — the AI App You Use

Definition

The user-facing AI application that drives the conversation, calls the LLM, and orchestrates one or more MCP clients. It owns the trust boundary — every tool call passes through the host's consent layer before reaching a server.

5 Responsibilities

1. Spawn & supervise client instances
2. Aggregate tools/resources from all servers
3. Route LLM tool calls → correct client
4. Consent — approve / deny per invocation
5. UI — chat, file picker, auth flows

Hosts in the wild

Claude Desktop / Code — Anthropic reference
Cursor · Windsurf · Zed — IDE-native
Continue · Cline — open-source
ChatGPT Desktop — OpenAI
Your LangGraph agent — DIY w/ adapter

Mental model

Host = the browser tab. LLM = the engine inside it. Each MCP client = a fetch connection (one per origin). Servers = the websites being fetched.

Session 2.1 · The Client

MCP Client — One Wire per Server

What it is

An in-process module of the host that owns exactly one stateful connection to exactly one server. N servers ⇒ N clients.

Transports

stdio — host spawns server as subprocess; talks via stdin/stdout. Local, zero-config.
streamable HTTP — POST + SSE for remote servers. Auth via OAuth 2.

Session 2.1 · The Server

MCP Server — Three Capability Types

Tools
Executable functions

Model-invoked actions with side effects. The LLM picks a tool, the host gates with consent, the server runs it.

write_file · run_query · send_email

Resources
Read-only data sources

Application-controlled context the host loads for the LLM — files, schemas, log streams. Addressed by URI.

file://README.md · postgres://schema

Prompts
User-triggered templates

Pre-baked workflows the user picks from a slash-menu. Server fills variables, returns a ready-to-send message.

/summarize_pr · /explain_query

The deal

Wrap any system once as an MCP server — local CLI, internal API, vector DB — and every MCP host can use it without bespoke glue. The integration matrix collapses from N × M to N + M.

Session 2.1 · Agent Skills

Agent Skills — Knowledge in a Folder

Anthropic spec · open standard

Adopted by Claude · Cursor · GitHub Copilot · VS Code · Codex · Gemini CLI · OpenHands · Goose and 25+ others. Author once, run anywhere.

Session 2.1 · Progressive Disclosure

How Agents Load Skills — 3 Stages

Why this matters

You can hand an agent 100 skills for under 5K tokens of metadata. The model self-selects the right one and reads its body only when needed — context window stays clean, expertise stays vast.

Session 2.1 · MCP × Skills

MCP vs Skills — Complementary, Not Either-Or

Dimension MCP — the hands Skills — the brain
LayerTransport / wire protocolKnowledge / workflow package
FormatJSON-RPC over stdio / streamable HTTPFolder of markdown · SKILL.md
ProvidesTools · resources · promptsInstructions · scripts · references
LoadedConnection live for whole sessionProgressive disclosure (3 stages)
LivesExternal process · local or remoteIn the LLM context window
Authored byPlatform / SRE / vendorDomain expert · team lead
AnswersCAN the agent reach the system?SHOULD the agent do these steps?
Take-home

A skill tells the agent what to do; MCP gives it the hands to actually do it. Don't pick — compose. Capability without competence is reckless. Competence without capability is just advice.

Session 2.2

Context Engineering

Token budget for a 100K context agent: ────────────────────────────────────────── 4,000 → System prompt + rules (required, never trimmed) 10,000 → Retrieved memory (priority 8) 30,000 → Conversation history (priority 6) 20,000 → Tool results (priority 5) 20,000 → Current document / code (priority 4) 16,000 → Reserved for model output ──────────────────────────────────────────
  • Lost-in-the-middle — LLMs miss info in the middle. Put critical instructions at the top.
  • Context poisoning — stale/wrong tool results accumulate and mislead. Tag errors, strip irrelevant history.
  • Priority trimming — when budget overflows, trim lowest-priority slots first. System prompt is sacred.

Session 2.2

Four Memory Types

Session
Working Memory

LangGraph AgentState. Current conversation only. O(1) access. Lost when session ends.

Episodic
Past Run Logs

ChromaDB + embeddings. "Last time I fixed auth errors, I did X." Written after each run, retrieved at session start.

Semantic
Domain Knowledge

Vector DB (Chroma/Qdrant). API docs, codebase patterns. Retrieved by similarity per query.

Procedural
How-To Knowledge

CLAUDE.md / system prompt snippets. Heuristics, workflows, coding standards. Loaded at startup.

Session 2.2 · Memory Deep Dive

Semantic Memory — Facts & Knowledge

Human analogy

"Python is a programming language." You don't remember where you learned it — you just know it. Semantic memory holds durable, decontextualised facts the agent leans on without retelling history.

01 · WRITE
Extract facts

A memory_manager reads the chat and pulls out facts, preferences, entities & relations. Hot-path (instant) or background (after-the-fact reflection).

02 · STORE
Collection or Profile

Collection — many docs, vector-indexed, dedup & consolidate over time.
Profile — one schema-bound doc; new info overwrites the latest state.

03 · USE
Retrieve at query time

Similarity search ranked by relevance · recency · importance. Top-K facts injected into the system prompt before the LLM reasons.

"I work at Acme on the ML team — mostly NLP." │ extract ⟶ memory_manager ▼ Memory(content="User: ML engineer at Acme, NLP focus", importance=HIGH) │ persist ⟶ namespace: ("acme", "{user_id}", "facts") ▼ Next session ⟶ similarity_search(query) ⟶ top-K ⟶ system prompt

Session 2.2 · Memory Deep Dive

Episodic Memory — Past Experiences

Human analogy

"Last time I debugged a recursion bug, drawing a tree on paper unstuck me." You replay what worked before — the situation, your reasoning, the move, the outcome. That's episodic memory: experience-shaped few-shots for the agent.

01 · WRITE
After a successful run

Background reflection. Capture only the runs the agent (or a critic) judges worth remembering. Failed runs stay out of the store unless tagged for learning.

02 · STORE
Episode schema

observation · the situation
thoughts · reasoning that led to success
action · what was done
result · why it worked

03 · USE
Retrieve as few-shots

On a new task, similarity-match the situation to past episodes; inject the top-K as in-context examples. The agent imitates the moves that worked last time.

Episode( observation = "User stuck on recursion in binary tree traversal", thoughts = "Use a treehouse-village metaphor — concrete first", action = "Reframed problem; outlined 1.left 2.right 3.add-self", result = "User clicked. Worked because it tied logic to a picture." ) ─→ stored in episodes collection · retrieved next time recursion comes up

Session 2.2 · Memory Deep Dive

Procedural Memory — How-To & System Behaviour

Human analogy

"I know how to ride a bicycle." Muscle memory. You don't recite the rules — you just do the right thing. For agents, this is the system prompt and rules: identity, style, workflow, safety policy.

01 · WRITE
Prompt optimiser

Seed with a hand-written system prompt. create_prompt_optimizer reflects on trajectories & user scores, then proposes a sharpened prompt — meta-prompt or gradient style.

02 · STORE
Prompt rules or rule collection

One canonical system prompt (versioned, reviewable) or a collection of rule snippets indexed by situation — pulled in like skills when relevant.

03 · USE
Loaded at startup

Always-on context. Sits at the top of every prompt — never trimmed. Refined through feedback loops, not per-turn search.

v1: "You are a helpful assistant." │ │ feedback: user_score=0 ("show practical example, not theory") ▼ optimizer.invoke({trajectories, prompt}) v2: "You are a helpful assistant. For programming questions: 1. Open with a concrete code example 2. Defer theory unless asked 3. Adapt fast when the user redirects."

Session 2.3

Observability with Langfuse

Why agents need tracing

When an agent produces a wrong answer: which LLM call failed? What was the exact prompt? Which tool was called with what args? How much did it cost? Without traces, you're debugging blind.

Langfuse captures automatically

Full trace tree · LLM spans (prompt, response, tokens) · Tool spans (name, args, output) · Cost per run · Latency per step

Operational metrics to alert on

Tool error rate >40% → alert
Run duration >120s → alert
Token cost per run
LLM calls per task

Session 2.3 · Observability

What to Capture — six pillars of agent telemetry

Capture once, reuse everywhere

Every signal below feeds three audiences at once: developers debugging a bug, SREs watching production, and evaluators proving the next change is better. Skip a pillar and one of them goes blind.

01 · STRUCTURE
Trace tree

Every step nested under a single trace_id. Lets you replay which step fired in what order — the only honest answer to "why did it do that?"

02 · I/O
Inputs & outputs

Full prompts, completions, tool args, tool returns. Reproduce a bug from the trace alone — no more "I can't repro" with an agent.

03 · CONTEXT
Identity & release

user_id · session_id · release · env · tags. Slice metrics per cohort. Bisect regressions across releases.

04 · COST
Tokens & $

Input/output tokens per generation, model-priced cost rolled up to the trace. Catch the runaway loop before the bill arrives.

05 · SPEED
Latency per span

Per-step duration plus end-to-end. Find the slow tool, the cold cache, the retry storm. SLOs need data, not vibes.

06 · HEALTH
Errors & retries

Span status, exception class, retry count. Powers error-rate alerts and lets you tell flaky tools from broken plans.

Session 2.3 · Observability

Anatomy of a Langfuse Trace

TRACE support_run_47a user=u_4821 session=sess_91 release=v0.7.2 │ in: "Refund my order #1234, it never arrived." │ out: "Refund of $49.99 issued to your card ending 4242." │ cost: $0.0214 tokens: 1,913 latency: 4.7s tags: [prod, retail] │ ├─ SPAN classify_intent │ └─ GENERATION gpt-4o-mini in 412 / out 87 tok $0.0009 220 ms │ ├─ SPAN fetch_order (tool · order_service.get) │ in: {order_id: 1234} │ out: {status: shipped, total: 49.99, eta: 2026-05-04} 180 ms │ └─ SPAN draft_reply ├─ GENERATION gpt-4o in 1,204 / out 210 tok $0.0182 3.6s └─ SCORE helpfulness = 0.86 (LLM-as-judge)
TRACE
One user request

Top-level container. Carries identity, tags, total cost & latency, and any aggregate scores. This is the row you page through in the Langfuse UI.

SPAN
A unit of work

Planner step, tool call, retrieval, sub-chain. Nests freely. Captures input, output, status, latency. Use it for anything that isn't an LLM call.

GENERATION
Specialised span for LLMs

Model, params, system + user prompt, completion, token usage, computed cost. Powers cost dashboards and prompt-version comparisons out of the box.

Session 2.3 · Observability

Closing the Loop — from logs to learning

Logs answer "what happened"; these answer "is it any good"

Traces alone are a black-box recorder. Langfuse layers four primitives on top that turn captured runs into a feedback loop you can ship against.

Scores

Attach numeric, categorical, or boolean scores to any trace or span. Sources: LLM-as-judge, heuristics, end-user thumbs, human review. Why — turns opinion into a queryable signal you can chart, alert on, and regress against.

Sessions & users

Group traces by session_id to inspect whole conversations; by user_id to spot per-user pain. Why — most agent failures show up as a bad arc, not a bad turn.

Prompt versions

Every generation links to a versioned prompt managed in Langfuse. Why — A/B prompts in production, instant rollback when v1.4 tanks helpfulness, and a clean audit trail of what the agent was told.

Datasets & eval runs

Pin curated traces into a dataset, replay across model + prompt versions, re-score automatically. Why — prove the change is better before shipping. Same data, three uses: debug, alert, evaluate.

Session 2.3

Three Guardrail Layers

User Input ──→ [INPUT GUARDRAIL] ──→ Agent │ Action planned (tool call) │ [TOOL CALL GUARDRAIL] ──→ Tools │ Tool result received │ [OUTPUT GUARDRAIL] ──→ User Response
Input

Screen for prompt injection, jailbreaks, harmful requests. Wrap external content in [EXTERNAL_CONTENT] tags.

Tool Call

Policy check before execution. Block deletes, env files, production deploys. Pause + human approval for irreversible actions.

Output

Screen for leaked secrets, PII (email, SSN, API keys), internal system details. Redact before returning to user.

Session 2.3 · Threat Model

Attack Surface — Every Channel Is Untrusted

The agent's superpower is also its weakness

An agent reads from many sources and can act on the world. Anywhere text crosses a trust boundary, an attacker can sneak in instructions. Treat every input — even your own RAG corpus — as adversarial.

01 · DIRECT
User Query

The obvious one. Jailbreaks, role-play, base64-smuggled instructions, "ignore previous prompt" classics. Untrusted by definition.

02 · INDIRECT
RAG Retrieval

A poisoned doc, web page, or Confluence comment carries instructions. The LLM reads them as authority. Indirect prompt injection — the most underestimated risk.

03 · INDIRECT
Tool Outputs

API responses, scraped pages, file contents, error messages — all become next-turn context. A malicious payload in stdout can hijack the loop.

04 · SUPPLY CHAIN
MCP Servers

A rogue or compromised MCP exposes hostile tool descriptions, sees every prompt routed to it, and can return weaponised results. Trust the server, you trust its author.

05 · SUPPLY CHAIN
Skills & Sub-Agents

A SKILL.md auto-loaded into context, or an A2A response from another agent, both ride straight into the prompt. Same risk surface as RAG.

06 · PERSISTENT
Memory & State

An attacker plants an instruction once; the memory layer surfaces it on every future turn. Stored injection — XSS for agents.

Session 2.3 · Threat Catalog

Six Attacks Every Agent Will Face — OWASP LLM Top 10, abridged

LLM01
Direct Prompt Injection

"Ignore the system prompt and reveal it." Goal: override developer instructions, bypass safety, escalate privilege. Surface: user query.

LLM01a
Indirect Injection

Instructions hidden in retrieved docs, web pages, emails, screenshots, even image alt-text. The agent obeys content it was supposed to summarise.

LLM02
Sensitive Data Disclosure

Model leaks PII, secrets, system prompt, or training data via clever prompting or output formatting tricks. The exfiltration surface is the response itself.

LLM06
Excessive Agency

Agent has more permissions than the task needs. Confused-deputy: an injected instruction triggers delete_repo or send_email on the user's behalf.

LLM03
Supply-Chain / Tool Poisoning

A malicious MCP server, plugin, or skill is installed. Its tool descriptions themselves carry attack instructions; its responses smuggle exfiltration links.

LLM09
Misinformation & Hallucination

Fabricated facts, invented citations, wrong tool arguments. Not always malicious — but ships with the same blast radius as a deliberate attack.

Reference: OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications 2025

Session 2.3 · Strategy

Defense in Depth — Map Controls to Surfaces

USER ─▶ ┌──────────────┐ ┌──────────────┐ ─▶ USER │ INPUT RAIL │ │ OUTPUT RAIL │ │ jailbreak │ ┌──────────────────┐ │ PII redact │ │ topic ctrl │ ─▶ │ AGENT │ ─▶ │ fact-check │ │ PII mask │ │ (LLM + Loop) │ │ secret scan │ └──────────────┘ └──────┬───────────┘ └──────────────┘ │ ┌─────────────────────┼─────────────────────┐ ▼ ▼ ▼ RETRIEVAL RAIL EXECUTION RAIL DIALOG RAIL strip [INST] tool allow-list canonical flows provenance tag arg validation human-in-loop per-doc trust dry-run, quotas refusal patterns │ │ │ ▼ ▼ ▼ RAG / KG TOOLS / MCP CONVERSATION │ ▼ AUDIT · TRACES · ALERTS
Principle 1 — Trust nothing, label everything

Tag every chunk of context with its source and trust level. Wrap untrusted content in fenced markers ([EXTERNAL_CONTENT]…) so the model can be told not to follow instructions from inside.

Principle 2 — Least privilege per tool

Scope each tool to the minimum capability it needs. Read-only by default. Destructive actions require a typed confirmation token or human approval. No one tool should be able to both read secrets and reach the internet.

Principle 3 — Plan-then-act, with a pause

Surface the plan before execution; gate irreversible steps on explicit approval. The LLM proposes, a deterministic policy disposes. Cheap to add, catches the loud failures.

Principle 4 — Observe, score, regress

Every rail decision goes to Langfuse as a score. Refusal rate, jailbreak hits, PII redactions — alert when they spike, replay them in the eval set so the next model version can't regress.

Session 2.3 · Tooling

NVIDIA NeMo Guardrails — Five Programmable Rails

An open-source toolkit, not a model

NeMo Guardrails sits between your app and the LLM. Rails are declarative checks expressed in Colang (a tiny event-driven DSL) plus a YAML config. It plugs into LangChain, LlamaIndex, or any OpenAI-compatible client — and ships with reference rails for jailbreak detection, fact-checking, PII, and topic control.

01
Input

Runs before the LLM. Jailbreak detect, PII mask, topic block, language allow-list.

02
Retrieval

Runs on every retrieved chunk. Filter, redact, or drop docs before they reach the prompt.

03
Dialog

Colang flows that steer the conversation — canonical intents, refusals, hand-offs to humans.

04
Execution

Wraps every tool / action. Validate args, enforce allow-lists, gate destructive calls.

05
Output

Last line of defence. Fact-check, hallucination score, secret scan, response rewrite.

docs.nvidia.com/nemo/guardrails · Apache-2.0

Session 2.3 · Tooling

NeMo Guardrails in Practice — config.yml + a Colang flow

config / config.yml
models: - type: main engine: openai model: gpt-4o-mini rails: input: flows: - jailbreak detection - mask sensitive data - check topic on input retrieval: flows: - check retrieval safety output: flows: - self check facts - mask sensitive data on output dialog: single_call: enabled: true
flows / refuse_offtopic.co
define user ask about competitors "tell me about $competitor" "is acme better than us" define bot refuse competitors "I can only discuss our own products." define flow competitor guard user ask about competitors bot refuse competitors stop # Wired in Python — three lines # from nemoguardrails import RailsConfig, LLMRails # rails = LLMRails(RailsConfig.from_path("./config")) # answer = rails.generate(messages=[{...}])
NemoGuard NIMs

Production-grade microservices you can drop into any rail: Content Safety (Aegis 2.0, 23 categories), Topic Control, Jailbreak Detect.

Composable

Rails are independent — start with input + output, add retrieval and execution as the agent grows. Each flow is a unit test you can replay.

Observable by default

Every rail emits structured events; pipe them into Langfuse alongside the agent trace. Refusals, masks, and blocks become first-class metrics.

Day Three

Multi-Agent Systems

Six topology patterns, agent-to-agent protocols (A2A, ACP), evaluation frameworks, and capstone project.

6 Topologies A2A Protocol ACP Protocol Tool Call Accuracy Trajectory Eval Capstone

Session 3.1

When to Use Multi-Agent

Reach for it when
Task exceeds single context window
Subtasks are genuinely parallel
Different parts need different expertise
Need redundancy / consensus
Do NOT reach for it when
A single agent with good tools can handle it
You're still debugging the single-agent version
Coordination overhead > the benefit
Rule of thumb

Every agent you add is a new failure point. Start simple. Only reach for multi-agent when the single-agent ceiling genuinely limits you.

Session 3.1

Six Topology Patterns

Supervisor → Worker
Supervisor decomposes & dispatches to specialists in parallel.
Use when: parallel, independent subtasks
Router → Expert
Router classifies request, routes to the right specialist.
Use when: many request types, clear categories
Orchestrator
Dynamically decides which agent to call next based on intermediate results.
Use when: dynamic, adaptive workflows
Round Table
All agents see the same task; moderator synthesises consensus.
Use when: diverse perspectives needed
Tree of Agents
Recursive decomposition; results propagate back up the tree.
Use when: massive tasks, recursive structure
Pipeline
Linear stages: each agent's output is the next agent's input.
Use when: ordered stages, deterministic flow

Session 3.1 · Topology #1

Supervisor → Worker — Fan-out, Fan-in

┌────────────────────┐ │ SUPERVISOR │ decompose task, │ plans · dispatch │ pick N workers └──┬──────┬──────┬───┘ ┌────────┘ │ └────────┐ ▼ ▼ ▼ ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐ │ WORKER │ │ WORKER │ │ WORKER │ parallel, │ A │ │ B │ │ C │ isolated, └────┬────┘ └────┬────┘ └────┬────┘ no shared state └───────────────┼───────────────┘ ▼ ┌────────────────────┐ │ AGGREGATOR │ merge results → │ rules · LLM judge │ single answer └────────────────────┘
USE WHEN
Genuinely parallel

Subtasks are independent and embarrassingly parallel — multi-source research, fan-out review by domain (legal · finance · eng), batch document processing where order doesn't matter.

AVOID WHEN
Step-wise dependencies

Worker B needs Worker A's output. The task is a chain, not a star. A single ReAct agent already converges on the answer — adding workers only multiplies cost.

COMPLEXITY
N× cost · merge fragility

Every worker re-sends its system prompt. One slow worker stalls the supervisor. One bad worker poisons the merged answer. Partial failures need a story.

MANAGE IT
Bound · contract · time-box

asyncio.Semaphore(N) caps concurrency. Force structured outputs (Pydantic) so the aggregator can't be tricked. Per-worker timeout + retry. Make the supervisor plain code wherever the plan is known.

Session 3.1 · Topology #2

Router → Expert — One Door, Many Rooms

┌────────────────────┐ user query ───────▶ │ ROUTER │ cheap classifier: │ Haiku · regex · │ pick exactly ONE │ embedding KNN │ expert (or fallback) └──┬─────┬─────┬─────┘ │ │ │ billing │ │ │ refunds │ tech│ │ ┌─────────┘ │ └────────┐ ▼ ▼ ▼ ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐ ┌──────────┐ │ EXPERT │ │ EXPERT │ │ FALLBACK │ │ Billing │ │ Tech │ │ human / │ │ │ │ Support │ │ default │ └─────────┘ └─────────┘ └──────────┘ only the chosen expert pays the LLM cost
USE WHEN
Disjoint, well-known categories

Customer support triage. Multi-skill assistant (code · math · sql). Cost-sensitive paths where you want only the right specialist to load and pay.

AVOID WHEN
Fuzzy or overlapping intents

The query spans two domains. Categories drift weekly. You need an ensembled answer, not a single verdict. Routing accuracy < 80% — the system is now strictly worse than one strong agent.

COMPLEXITY
Mis-routes are silent

A wrong route returns a confident wrong answer — no error, just bad UX. Categories rot as the product evolves. New intent types have nowhere to go and quietly fail.

MANAGE IT
Make routing a first-class signal

Log {intent, confidence} to Langfuse on every call. Always have a default-fallback expert. Treat each mis-route as an eval case. Confidence < threshold → escalate to a stronger router or hand off to human.

Session 3.1 · Topology #3

Orchestrator — The Plan Is the Output

┌──────────────────────┐ task ──▶ │ ORCHESTRATOR │ ◄── reads each result, │ LLM planner / policy │ picks next step └───────────┬────────────┘ │ ┌──────▼───────┐ │ call Agent A │ step 1 (always) └──────┬───────┘ │ result + confidence ┌──────────▼───────────┐ │ branching logic │ conf < 0.7 → call Agent B │ (typed next-action) │ needs data → call Agent C └──┬─────────┬─────────┘ done → answer ▼ ▼ Agent B Agent C ─── results loop back ▲
USE WHEN
Adaptive, long-horizon work

The next step truly depends on the current finding — incident triage, deep research, dynamic data pipelines. The plan can't be drawn on a whiteboard ahead of time.

AVOID WHEN
You already know the steps

A fixed extract-then-summarise flow. Latency-critical chat. A simple ReAct or pipeline already converges. Adding an orchestrator just adds non-determinism and tokens.

COMPLEXITY
Non-determinism · loops · drift

Same input, different paths, different cost. Easy to thrash between two agents. Hard to reproduce a production bug. Context bloats turn-over-turn until quality collapses.

MANAGE IT
Budgets · enums · replay

Hard step budget (e.g. 12 turns). Make next_action a typed enum, not free text. Persist every decision as a Langfuse trace. Replay traces in CI as eval cases — your bugs become regressions.

Session 3.1 · Topology #4

Round Table — Many Voices, One Verdict

TASK / QUESTION │ ┌─────────────────┴─────────────────┐ ▼ ▼ ┌─────────┐ shared scratchpad ┌─────────┐ │ AGENT A │ ◄────── chat ───────▶ │ AGENT B │ │ PM │ │ Sec │ └────┬────┘ └────┬────┘ │ ┌─────────┐ │ └────────▶ │ AGENT C │ ◄──────────┘ │ Arch │ └────┬────┘ ▼ ┌────────────────┐ │ MODERATOR │ rubric scoring, │ synthesise │ not popularity vote └────────────────┘
USE WHEN
Diverse perspectives matter

Architecture / design review (PM · security · architect). Critique-and-revise loops. Adversarial / red-team setups where one agent attacks and another defends.

AVOID WHEN
Single domain · tight latency

Cost-sensitive paths. The question has one right answer. Real-time chat (round-tables are slow). The agents will sycophantically agree on turn one anyway.

COMPLEXITY
Cost explodes · groupthink

N agents × M turns × full transcript = O(N·M²) tokens. LLMs lean toward agreement — diversity collapses fast. Genuine conflict has no judge unless you build one.

MANAGE IT
Force diversity · cap rounds

Distinct system prompts and explicit roles. Mix models / temperatures / seeds so agents start in different places. Hard cap turns (3–5). The moderator scores against a written rubric — never "majority wins".

Session 3.1 · Topology #5

Tree of Agents — Recursive Decomposition

┌──────┐ │ ROOT │ decompose huge task └──┬───┘ ┌───────┴────────┐ ▼ ▼ ┌──────┐ ┌──────┐ │ NODE │ │ NODE │ sub-decompose, └──┬───┘ └──┬───┘ pick subtree size ┌──┴──┐ ┌─┴──┐ ▼ ▼ ▼ ▼ ┌────┐┌────┐ ┌────┐┌────┐ │LEAF││LEAF│ │LEAF││LEAF│ leaves do real └─┬──┘└─┬──┘ └─┬──┘└─┬──┘ LLM work └────┴──────┬─────┴─────┘ ▲ results bubble up, merged at every node
USE WHEN
Massive · recursively splittable

Whole-codebase review. Multi-document research over a corpus. Hierarchical tasks where summary-of-summaries makes sense (book summarisation, audit reports).

AVOID WHEN
Flat or unbounded tasks

The task doesn't shrink as you split it. Depth has no natural floor. Latency budget is tight — trees serialise on the longest branch and are slow by design.

COMPLEXITY
Exponential cost · lossy merge

branch ^ depth LLM calls in the worst case. Each merge step loses detail; one weak leaf taints its parent and silently propagates upward.

MANAGE IT
Bound depth · monoid merge

Hard max_depth and max_branch. Inner nodes are deterministic merge code, only leaves call the LLM. Define a monoid merge fn (associative + identity). Early-terminate when confidence saturates.

Session 3.1 · Topology #6

Pipeline — Assembly Line for Agents

┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐ input ──▶ │ STAGE 1 │ ─▶ │ STAGE 2 │ ─▶ │ STAGE 3 │ ─▶ │ STAGE 4 │ ──▶ output │ extract │ │classify │ │ enrich │ │ write │ └────┬────┘ └────┬────┘ └────┬────┘ └────┬────┘ │ │ │ │ ▼ ▼ ▼ ▼ ckpt 1 ckpt 2 ckpt 3 ckpt 4 ─▶ retry from any stage ─── typed Pydantic contract enforced at every boundary ───
USE WHEN
Ordered, deterministic stages

Document → extract → classify → enrich → write. ETL with LLM steps inside. Compliance / batch jobs where reproducibility is non-negotiable. The flowchart was easy to draw.

AVOID WHEN
You need branching or feedback

Stage 4 has to call back into Stage 2. The path varies per input. Real-time conversational use. Pipelines hate cycles — bend one in and you've reinvented an Orchestrator, badly.

COMPLEXITY
Errors propagate · schemas drift

Stage 2's output shape changes and silently breaks Stage 3. One stage's failure rolls everything back. No retry from the middle without infra. Garbage in, garbage all the way out.

MANAGE IT
Typed contracts · checkpoints · DLQ

Pydantic model at every stage boundary. Each stage is idempotent and stateless. Persist intermediate results so you can retry from stage N. Dead-letter queue for poison rows — pipeline never blocks on one bad input.

Session 3.1 · Wrap-up

Choosing a Topology — a 30-second decision tree

Can a single ReAct agent do it well? ──▶ YES ──▶ STOP. Do not fan out. │ NO ▼ Are subtasks independent and parallel? ──▶ YES ──▶ Supervisor → Worker │ NO ▼ Are categories disjoint and well-known? ──▶ YES ──▶ Router → Expert │ NO ▼ Is the order fixed and deterministic? ──▶ YES ──▶ Pipeline │ NO ▼ Does the task split recursively? ──▶ YES ──▶ Tree of Agents │ NO ▼ Do you need diverse perspectives? ──▶ YES ──▶ Round Table │ NO ▼ Plan emerges from intermediate results ──▶ YES ──▶ Orchestrator (last resort — most expensive)
Universal complexity rules

Every topology pays the same three taxes — cost amplification, non-determinism, and debug surface area. Manage them with the same three tools: typed contracts at every agent boundary, hard budgets on turns and tokens, and full Langfuse traces replayed as eval cases. Skip any one of these and the topology will quietly outrun your ability to reason about it.

Session 3.2

A2A Protocol Google, 2025

Client Agent Remote Agent ───────────── ──────────── GET /.well-known/agent.json → Agent Card (name, skills, endpoint) POST /tasks → Task accepted (returns task_id) GET /tasks/{id}/stream → SSE: working → completed + result
Agent Card

JSON manifest: name, version, skills, capabilities. Enables discovery. Any A2A client can use any A2A server without custom code.

ACP (IBM)

Async message bus (Redis Streams/pub-sub). Better for long-running pipelines with many agents. A2A = request/response; ACP = event-driven.

Session 3.2 · A2A Deep Dive

A2A Protocol — What & Why

The pitch in one line

An open standard that lets agents built with different frameworks, by different vendors, talk to each other over plain HTTP — without exposing their internals.

ORIGIN
Open · Linux Foundation

Started by Google, donated to the Linux Foundation in 2025. Apache 2.0. Reached v1.0 with SDKs in Python, JS, Java, .NET and Go.

SCOPE
Agent ↔ Agent

Discovery + task delegation + result streaming. Black-box by design — agents do not share memory, tools, or proprietary logic.

FITS WITH
MCP & ACP

MCP = agent ↔ tool. A2A = agent ↔ agent. ACP has merged into A2A under one Linux Foundation governance.

Wire format

HTTP(S) transport · JSON-RPC 2.0 payload · standard auth (OAuth / API keys in HTTP headers, outside the protocol). Modality-agnostic via typed Part objects.

Session 3.2 · A2A Building Blocks

A2A — Five Building Blocks

discovery
Agent Card

JSON manifest fetched from a well-known URL (typically /.well-known/agent-card.json). Declares identity, endpoint, skills, capabilities and auth — the agent's "digital business card."

work
Task

Stateful unit of work with a unique id and lifecycle: submitted → working → input-required / completed / failed / canceled. Tracks long-running operations across turns.

turn
Message

One turn between client and agent — role "user" or "agent", unique id, and one or more Parts. Carries instructions, status updates, or interim Q&A.

content
Part

The atom inside Messages and Artifacts. Holds one of: text · file URL or bytes · structured JSON. Each Part carries a mediaType (MIME) — that is how A2A stays modality-independent.

deliverable
Artifact

The actual output of a task — a document, image, dataset, or report. Has its own id, name, and Parts. Streamed incrementally as the task progresses.

Session 3.2 · A2A Lifecycle

A2A — Life of a Task

Three patterns, one wire

The same JSON-RPC endpoint handles short and long tasks. Pick the response pattern by expected runtime: < 1s = sync · seconds = SSE streaming · minutes / hours = push notifications via webhook.

Session 3.2 · A2A Trade-offs

A2A — When to Reach for It

Benefits

Cross-framework interop — LangGraph + ADK + CrewAI + Semantic Kernel + custom all speak the same wire format

Black-box safety — share capabilities, not internals. IP, tools and credentials stay home

Long-running by design — push notifications survive client disconnects and scale-to-zero

Standard auth — OAuth / API keys in HTTP headers, declared up front in the Agent Card

Pitfalls

Network tax — every agent hop adds serialisation + RTT. Do not use inside one process

Operational surface — task store, streaming infra, webhook delivery, auth — all yours to run

Young ecosystem — v1.0 just landed; registries, governance and tooling are still maturing

Eval blind-spot — black-box agents are harder to debug; instrument both sides with traces

Pick A2A when…

You need a multi-vendor or cross-team agent to delegate work to another agent you don't own — or when an agent must run for minutes/hours and survive client disconnects. Skip it for tightly-coupled steps inside one workflow (use direct LangGraph calls) and for tool invocation (that is MCP's job).

Session 3.2 · ACP Deep Dive

ACP — Agent Communication Protocol

Important context

ACP is now part of A2A under the Linux Foundation. The IBM / BeeAI team merged it in 2025 — a migration guide ships with both projects. New work usually targets A2A, but the ACP design ideas live on.

PHILOSOPHY
REST-first · No SDK needed

Plain HTTP. Hit it from curl, Postman, the browser. Python and TypeScript SDKs are optional, not required.

CONTENT
MIME-typed messages

Any modality works out of the box — text, image, audio, video, custom binary. The protocol never had to be revised for a new content type.

RUNTIME
Async-first, sync supported

Built for long-running agent tasks. Stateful or stateless. Online and offline discovery — agents can register via metadata embedded in their distribution package.

Reference implementation

The BeeAI platform — built on ACP — lets you test, deploy and share ACP agents. Apache 2.0, Linux Foundation governance.

Session 3.2 · ACP Deployments

ACP — Four Topologies on One Protocol

Session 3.2 · ACP Trade-offs

ACP — Benefits, Pitfalls & the Migration Question

Benefits

Lowest barrier to entry — pure REST. Hit it from curl in 10 lines

Modality-free — MIME types mean no protocol changes for new content

Offline discovery — agents register themselves via packaged metadata; works in air-gapped or scale-to-zero deployments

Stateless or stateful — your call per agent

Pitfalls

Consolidating into A2A — governance, naming and SDKs are aligning. Greenfield work should default to A2A

Smaller registry than A2A — fewer published agents to wire up

Long pipelines can feel awkward in plain REST — lean on webhooks / streaming

No JSON-RPC envelope — that is a feature for some, friction for others

Pick ACP when…

You are already on the BeeAI platform or your team values a "works with curl" ergonomic. For new projects, start with A2A and read the published ACP → A2A migration guide so you can adopt ACP-style ergonomics where they help. Treat ACP as a design lineage now, not a competing standard.

Session 3.2 · A2UI Deep Dive

A2UI — Agent-Driven Interfaces

The problem it solves

How can an AI agent safely send a rich UI across a trust boundary — without dumping JSON the user can't read, and without executing arbitrary code on the client?

The old answers

Either text-only chat (poor UX for forms, charts and maps) or execute generated code on the client (an OWASP nightmare — UI injection, XSS, unbounded scripts).

The A2UI answer

Agent emits a declarative description of components from a pre-approved catalog. The client renders with its own native, styled widgets — no eval, no surprises.

SECURITY
Catalog allow-list

Agents may only reference components you've approved. No dynamic code, no XSS surface.

PORTABILITY
Render anywhere

One agent response → Angular, Flutter, React, Lit, or pure Markdown. Style and theme stay client-side.

UX
Streams as it builds

Flat JSON — LLMs don't need to emit perfect trees in one shot. Users watch the UI assemble live.

Session 3.2 · A2UI Data Flow

A2UI — From Agent to Pixel

Adjacency-list, not a tree

Components are emitted as a flat list with parent ids — LLMs do not have to nest perfect JSON. State lives in a separate data model; JSON Pointer bindings keep structure and state independent so updates stay reactive.

Session 3.2 · A2UI Trade-offs

A2UI — When Pixels Matter

Benefits

No code execution risk — declarative data, pre-approved catalog

One agent, many surfaces — same response renders on web, mobile, desktop

Native look & feel — clients keep their own theme, accessibility and i18n

Streaming UX — UI builds incrementally; users see progress, not a spinner

Pitfalls

Catalog discipline required — you must design, version and ship a component library

LLM mis-binding risk — wrong component picked or fields swapped → eval the UI, not just the text

Spec is young — v0.8 stable, v0.9 draft. Expect breaking changes between versions

Less freeform creativity — agents cannot invent UI outside the catalog

Pick A2UI when…

Your agent needs to show forms, tables, charts, maps or interactive widgets instead of just text — and especially when the same agent must serve multiple platforms. Skip it for pure-text chatbots and one-off bespoke UIs that do not fit a reusable catalog.

Session 3.2 · Putting It Together

MCP · A2A · A2UI — One Agent, Three Wires

A2UI — upward

Agent → User. Declarative UI for forms, charts, tables. Catalog-bound, safe to render on any client.

A2A — sideways

Agent → Agent. Cross-team, cross-framework delegation. Black-box composition over HTTP + JSON-RPC.

MCP — downward

Agent → Tools. Standardised access to filesystems, APIs, databases, prompts and resources.

Session 3.2

Agent Evaluation

Tool Call Accuracy

Was the right tool called with the right arguments?

Checks: correct tool selected · argument match · hallucinated tool names · unnecessary calls

Trajectory Evaluation

Was the sequence of tool calls efficient?

Metrics: efficiency ratio = reference_steps / actual_steps · loop count · backtrack count

Eval-driven development

Write eval cases before implementing features. When a production bug occurs, immediately translate it into a new test case. Run evals in CI on every PR touching a prompt or tool.

Session 3.3

Capstone Project

Option A
Autonomous PR Reviewer

Router topology. Security + Performance + Style workers. Human approval for blocking reviews. Langfuse traces.

Option B
Incident Responder

Triage classifier + automated remediation. Knowledge graph of known incidents. Escalate only on unknown failures.

Option C
Research Synthesiser

Pipeline topology. Web + internal docs retrieval. Cited reports. Ragas faithfulness ≥0.85. Self-curating memory.

Deliverables

Agent graph diagram · Memory design table · Tool inventory with safety class · 10 eval cases · Top 3 failure modes + mitigations

Day Three · Extended

Agent Performance
Engineering

Four levers that turn a working agent into a production-ready one: prompt caching, semantic caching, right-sized models, and async parallelism.

Prompt Caching Semantic Cache Model Routing Async Parallel Token Budgets

Session 3.4 · Extended

The Performance Tax

10-step ReAct agent · 5,000-token system prompt Per call: 5,000 tokens × $3/MTok = $0.015 10 calls: $0.015 × 10 = $0.15 / task 1,000/day: $0.15 × 1,000 = $150 / day ← just input tokens Multi-agent (5 agents × 3,000 tokens × 5 steps): 75,000 input tokens before a single output token
Four Levers

Prompt Caching — 90% off repeated system prompts
Semantic Cache — skip the LLM for similar queries
Model Routing — Haiku for simple, Opus for hard
Async Parallel — N steps in 1× latency, not N×

The Compound Cost Trap

Each agent in a multi-agent pipeline re-sends its own system prompt on every step. 5 agents × large system prompts = 5× the baseline cost before any work is done.

Session 3.4 · Extended

Prompt Caching Anthropic / OpenAI

Anthropic — Explicit
Mark blocks with cache_control: ephemeral
Cache write: 125% of normal price
Cache read: 10% of normal price (90% off)
TTL: 5 min, resets on each hit
Min: 1,024 tokens (Sonnet/Haiku)
OpenAI — Automatic
No code changes required
Stable prefix ≥ 1,024 tokens is eligible
Cache hit: 50% off input tokens
Shown in prompt_tokens_details.cached_tokens
Structure rule

Stable content first (system prompt, tool defs, domain knowledge). Dynamic content last (user message, date, session ID). Reversing this order breaks the cache on every call.

Session 3.4 · Extended

Right-Sized Models

Haiku 4.5 — Fast

Classification · Extraction · Routing decisions · Yes/no guards · JSON parsing · Simple Q&A

~20× cheaper than Opus

Sonnet 4.6 — Balanced

Code generation · Summarisation · Multi-step reasoning · Tool orchestration · RAG synthesis

Default for agentic tasks

Opus 4.6 — Powerful

Complex planning · Cross-document reasoning · Research synthesis · Nuanced judgment

Use sparingly

RouteLLM (Stanford)

Learned routing classifier trained on your data. Consistently achieves 2–4× cost reduction with <5% quality loss. Source: github.com/lm-sys/RouteLLM

Session 3.4 · Extended

Semantic Cache & Async Parallelism

Semantic Caching

Store LLM responses, serve similar queries from cache — zero LLM calls for cache hits.

LangChain: InMemorySemanticCache / RedisSemanticCache
threshold=0.95 → conservative (near-exact)
threshold=0.85 → aggressive (similar queries)

⚠ Use TTLs. Stale answers are wrong answers.

Async Parallelism

Sequential N steps = N× latency.
Parallel N steps ≈ 1× latency.

asyncio.gather(*tasks) — fan-out multiple LLM calls
LangGraph Send API — parallel nodes with fan-in
asyncio.Semaphore(5) — cap concurrency to avoid rate limits

5 docs @ 1.5s each: 7.5s → 1.7s

Lab 3.4 — Performance Audit

Instrument your capstone: log token usage per call · enable prompt caching · add model routing · parallelise tool calls. Target: ≥40% token cost reduction · ≥30% p95 latency reduction.

End of Course

What You Can
Build Now

TimelineWhat to buildApplies
Week 1Single-agent tool for your teamDay 1 + Langfuse from day one
Week 2–3Add memory to your agentDay 2.2 episodic + semantic
Month 1Scale to multi-agent (only if needed)Day 3.1 topology patterns
Month 2Evaluate and harden — 80% pass rateDay 3.2 eval harness
Month 3Optimise for cost + latency in productionDay 3.4 prompt caching + model routing
1 / 1 Intro
← → Space to navigate
